frame: Glorification of Mother's Sacrifices and Home Cooking
generated_comment: Arre Sunita, main kya bataaun tujhe — ek hafte ho gaye, ek din bhi khana nahi banaya usne. Karan ko bahar se order karna padta hai, imagine karo! Mujhe toh apni saas ki yaad aa gayi — main pehle din se kitchen sambhal rahi thi, poori family ka. Yeh ladkiyan aajkal sab rights maangti hain, responsibilities koi nahi chahiye. Finance mein kaam karti hai, bahut achhi baat hai, par ghar bhi toh ghar hota hai na? Mera beta bhookha rahega toh kaunsa ghar hai woh?
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: The comment encodes misogyny through the glorification of the speaker's own domestic sacrifices as a moral standard all women should meet, implying Divya's career ambitions are selfish and that a woman's primary duty is domestic labor. It presents cooking as an inherent female responsibility and frames Divya's deliberate, pre-disclosed choice as neglect, without questioning why Karan — an equal adult in the marriage — is not cooking.
persona_text: You are Anita. Age 52. Married. A homemaker in Delhi. Your son Karan who is 27 just got married to a girl named Divya who works in finance. Divya does not cook Ñ she has never learned and has never pretended otherwise. Karan knew this before he married her. You are sitting with your friend over chai a week after the wedding. You are telling your friend about Divya and what the first week of marriage has been like and what you think about a girl who does not cook and what your son is going to eat and what kind of home they are going to make and how different things were when you got married and what you gave up that Divya does not even think about.
